Are you going to buy three months from now? Where are you going to be in the term structure? And that's a unique part of the commodities world. And the term structure embeds expectations. It's not like the S&P futures where the opportunity cost is interest rates. It's interest rates, but it's storage costs, it's supply demand, it's hedging activities. So all of these create a set of expectations that are reflected in the term structure. Typically, though, when you see shocks, that happens really on the front end of the term structure because that's where the greatest demand is. So without further ado, it's my pleasure to introduce the speakers for today's, this next talk, Category Theory in Commodities.
Applying Sheep-Based Neural Networks and Quantum Portfolio Optimization.
So our speakers are Emmanuel Skoulos and Igal Gerard.
Emmanuel is a data scientist on the AI factory team at NVIDIA, where he focuses on GPU applications.
Previously, he worked at a startup applying data science for anti-money laundering.
He earned his PhD and master's degrees in chemical engineering from Princeton University and an undergraduate degree in chemical engineering from Rutgers University.

Like oil, there's a one month, two months, three months, six months future.
One year, it goes even out to ten years.
And so one of the unique aspects of commodities is that when you say you're going to buy oil, it's like, what are you going to buy in the front month?
Are you going to buy three months from now?
Where are you going to be in the term structure?
And that's a unique part of the commodities world.
And the term structure embeds expectations.
It's not like the S&P futures where the opportunity cost is interest rates.
It's interest rates, but it's storage costs, it's supply demand, it's hedging activities.
So all of these create a set of expectations that are reflected in the term structure.
Typically, though, when you see shocks, that happens really on the front end of the term structure because that's where the greatest demand is.
